1: Chapter 39 is the second and concluding half of the Gog-Magog oracle that began in Ezekiel 38. The oracle is the longest single prophetic unit in Ezekiel (Ezekiel 38-39, 58 verses) and serves as the climax of the restoration section (Ezekiel 33-39). After Ezekiel 38’s buildup of Gog’s invasion, Ezekiel 39 describes YHWH’s decisive intervention, the aftermath of the battle, and the theological meaning of the event for Israel and the nations. The command “prophesy against Gog” repeats Ezekiel 38:2, creating a structural envelope. The identification of Gog remains debated: a historical nation, an eschatological cipher, or a symbolic representative of all anti-God powers.
2: “I will turn you back and drive you forward” (vishuvtecha veshishshetika) — the verb shashah may mean “drive you forward” or “entice you onward” toward Israel. The phrase signals that YHWH is not a passive observer of Gog’s invasion but its author — he is bringing Gog against Israel for his own purposes (Ezekiel 38:16: “that the nations may know me”). “From the remotest north” (meyarketei tzafon) — the phrase appears in Isaiah 14:13 and Psalms 48:2 as the location of the divine mountain; it marks Gog’s origin as the far north, the direction associated with the entry of invaders into Israel throughout the prophetic corpus.
3: “I will strike your bow from your left hand and make your arrows drop from your right hand” — the military equipment Gog brought for war is instantly rendered useless by direct divine action. The bow and arrow imagery connects to Ezekiel 38:4 (Gog is brought with bows, shields, swords) and deliberately reverses the threat: the weapons that were supposed to destroy Israel are disarmed before a single engagement. The rendering harmless of Gog’s weapons without a conventional battle emphasizes that the victory is entirely YHWH’s, without Israel’s military contribution.
4: “You will fall on the mountains of Israel” — the mountains of Israel are the geographic and theological heart of the covenant land. The birds of prey consuming Gog’s army inverts the nations’ expectation: the invaders who came to plunder Israel become the prey. The formula “to birds of prey of every kind and to the wild animals” anticipates the great feast oracle of Ezekiel 39:17-20 and echoes Deuteronomy 28:26 (covenant curse: “your carcasses shall be food for all the birds”) — Gog’s army receives the covenant-curse destined for Israel’s enemies.
5: “You will fall in the open field, for I have spoken, declares the Lord GOD” (neum Adonai YHWH). The oracle formula “for I have spoken” grounds the certainty of Gog’s fall not in Israel’s military power or geopolitical advantage but in the irrevocability of YHWH’s word. The “open field” (al-penei hasadeh) — unburied in the open, exposed to scavengers — is the ultimate indignity in the ancient Near Eastern honor culture, the reverse of honorable burial.
6: “I will send fire on Magog and on those who dwell securely in the coastlands” — the judgment extends beyond Gog’s army to Magog itself (the homeland) and to the “coastlands” (iyyim), the distant maritime nations. The fire (esh) is the standard OT symbol for divine judgment (Amos 1-2; Isaiah 29:6; Revelation 8:5). The inclusion of the “coastlands” signals that Gog represents a coalition of world powers — the judgment is not merely regional but universal in scope. “They will know that I am the LORD” — the recognition formula marks the theological purpose of even this remote judgment.
7: “I will make my holy name known in the midst of my people Israel, and I will not let my holy name be profaned anymore; and the nations will know that I am the LORD, the Holy One in Israel.” The name-theology that governed Ezekiel 20’s historical survey reaches its eschatological resolution: the profanation of YHWH’s name that resulted from Israel’s exile (Ezekiel 36:20: “these are YHWH’s people, but they went out of his land”) will never recur. “The Holy One in Israel” (qadosh beyisrael) — YHWH’s holiness is displayed not only among the nations but within the covenant community.
8: “Behold, it has come and it has happened, declares the Lord GOD. This is the day of which I have spoken.” The performative declaration — “it has come and it has happened” (hinei vaat uveneta) — gives the oracle a prophetic-perfect quality: the future event is described as already accomplished from the divine perspective. “The day of which I have spoken” connects to the “day of the LORD” tradition in the prophetic corpus (Amos 5:18-20; Joel 2:1; Isaiah 13:6; Zephaniah 1:14-15) — Gog’s defeat is the eschatological day YHWH has long announced.
9: “The inhabitants of the cities of Israel will go out and make fires with the weapons and burn them” — the seven-year weapon-burning oracle (Ezekiel 39:9-10) describes the aftermath of Gog’s defeat in terms that reverse the normal flow of plunder. Israel burns the abandoned weapons for fuel rather than chopping wood from forests — indicating both the massive scale of Gog’s army (enough weapons for seven years of fuel) and Israel’s security: they go out from their cities to gather weapons rather than fleeing before enemies. The seven-year duration echoes the seven-month burial period (Ezekiel 39:12) — both numbers suggest completeness and thoroughness of cleansing.
10: “They will not need to take wood from the fields or cut any from the forests, for they will fuel their fires with the weapons.” The motif of not needing to work the forests for fuel suggests economic restoration — the land’s normal labor is replaced by the abundance of captured war material. “They will plunder those who plundered them and loot those who looted them” — the reversal formula echoes Isaiah 14:2 (“they will plunder those who plundered them”) and the covenant-curse reversal of Deuteronomy 30:7. The nations that came to despoil Israel become the source of Israel’s wealth.
11: “On that day I will give Gog a burial place in Israel — the Valley of the Travelers, east of the sea.” The Valley of the Travelers (gei ha-overim) — also called Hamon-gog (Ezekiel 39:15: “multitude of Gog”) — is east of the Dead Sea (the “sea” in Ezekiel typically refers to the Mediterranean or the Dead Sea depending on context; the location east of a sea suggests the Transjordanian region or the valley east of the Dead Sea). “It will block the path of travelers” — the massive burial site is so large it obstructs the normal transit routes, a dramatic symbol of the scale of Gog’s defeat.
12: “The house of Israel will spend seven months burying them in order to cleanse the land.” The seven-month burial period reflects the priestly concern for land-purity: unburied corpses defiled the land under Israelite law (Numbers 19:11-16; Deuteronomy 21:23). The massive undertaking — all of Israel engaged in cleansing the land for seven months — mirrors the Levitical purification procedures scaled to eschatological proportions. The number seven is significant: it corresponds to the sabbatical cycle and suggests complete, thoroughgoing cleansing, not merely partial tidying.
13: “All the people of the land will bury them, and it will bring them renown on the day I am glorified, declares the Lord GOD.” The burying of Gog’s dead — normally a humbling task — becomes an occasion for Israel’s honor (shem, renown, name). The contrast with Gog’s dishonor (falling in the open field, Ezekiel 39:5) is sharp: Israel gains renown precisely by performing the act of burying those who came to destroy them. “On the day I am glorified” (beyom hikavdi) — the burial of Gog’s army is simultaneously an act that brings glory to YHWH through Israel’s obedient service.
14: The logistics of the burial operation: a permanent detail of men is appointed to search the land for remaining human bones after the initial seven-month mass burial. “After seven months they will begin their search” — the search-and-marker protocol of Ezekiel 39:14-16 reflects the OT’s priestly concern for complete land-purification. The ongoing work underscores the thoroughness of the cleansing: even individual bones scattered in remote areas must be found and buried, and no one should stumble accidentally upon human remains and incur uncleanness.
15: “Whenever any of those traveling through the land sees a human bone, they are to set up a marker beside it until the burial teams have buried it in the Valley of Hamon-gog.” The marker-system reflects the priestly protocol of Numbers 19:16-19 — anyone who touches a human bone in the open becomes unclean for seven days. The civilian marker-system enlists the general population in the purification effort: ordinary travelers who spot a bone do not bury it themselves (risking uncleanness) but flag it for the burial detail. The systematic, careful thoroughness of the post-battle cleansing emphasizes the complete removal of all that is associated with death and judgment.
16: “There will also be a city there called Hamonah.” (gam shem-ir hamonah): the city named Hamonah (from hamon, multitude) commemorates the site of Gog’s burial. The founding of a city named “Multitude” at the site of the mass burial is an ironic monument: the city’s very name preserves the memory of the great multitude defeated by YHWH. The naming of the valley (Ezekiel 39:11: Hamon-gog) and the city (Ezekiel 39:16: Hamonah) creates a permanent memorial — the landscape itself becomes a witness to YHWH’s victory over all nations who rise against Israel.
17: Verses 17-20 form the “great sacrifice of YHWH” — a deliberate parody of the covenant sacrificial feast. YHWH invites birds and beasts to a zevach (sacrifice) on the mountains of Israel. The irony is sharp: normally a sacrifice involves animals slaughtered and offered to God; here God’s “sacrifice” is the slaughtered army of Gog, consumed by scavengers. The same imagery appears in Revelation 19:17-18 (“the great supper of God”), which directly draws on this passage. The invitation to “every kind of bird and every wild animal” mirrors the invitation formula of a royal feast.
18: “You will eat the flesh of the mighty and drink the blood of the earth’s princes — rams, lambs, and goats, bulls, all the fatlings of Bashan.” The military elite of Gog’s coalition are described in terms of sacrificial animals — rams, lambs, goats, bulls. Bashan (in the Golan Heights north of Israel) was famous for its well-fed livestock (Psalms 22:12; Amos 4:1). The “fatlings of Bashan” were the finest sacrificial animals; describing Gog’s leaders as “fatlings of Bashan” makes their consumption the consummation of a sacrifice of the highest quality — from YHWH’s perspective, the greatest and most powerful of the nations are merely cattle for slaughter when they oppose him.
19: “You will eat fat until you are gorged and drink blood until you are drunk.” The imagery intensifies to a graphic description of total consumption. Blood-drinking was strictly prohibited to Israel (Leviticus 17:14; Genesis 9:4) — only scavenging animals drink blood freely. The reversal: the nations who came bearing weapons against Israel become the nourishment of the natural world’s scavengers. The “sacrifice that I have prepared for you” (mizivchi asher zavachti lachem) establishes YHWH as the host and Gog’s army as the meal — a complete inversion of the nations’ militaristic expectations.
20: “You will be filled at my table with horses and chariots, with mighty warriors and every kind of soldier, declares the Lord GOD.” The divine table (shulchani) at which scavengers feast on Gog’s army echoes and inverts the table of the Lord’s Supper: where the NT meal feeds the community with Christ’s body and blood, this eschatological feast feeds the creation with the bodies of those who opposed YHWH. The listing of “horses and chariots, mighty warriors and every kind of soldier” exhausts the categories of military force — no element of Gog’s military power survives.
21: “I will display my glory among the nations, and all the nations will see my judgment that I have carried out, and my hand that I have laid on them.” The Gog oracle’s theological purpose — the display of YHWH’s glory among the nations — is now explicitly stated. The recognition formula shifts to a universal scope: “all the nations will see.” YHWH’s “hand” (yad) is the standard OT expression for his powerful historical interventions (the same word used for the exodus: “with a mighty hand”). Gog’s defeat is an event of world-historical significance that announces YHWH’s sovereignty to every nation.
22: “From that day forward, the house of Israel will know that I am the LORD their God.” The recognition formula applied specifically to Israel marks the Gog battle as the turning point of Israel’s history: after this day, the sustained unbelief and apostasy that characterized the historical survey of Ezekiel 20 is over. “From that day forward” (miyom hahu vahaleah) is a decisive temporal marker signaling the end of Israel’s pre-restoration ignorance and the beginning of permanent covenant knowledge — the experiential fulfillment of Jeremiah 31:34 (“they shall all know me”).
23: “The nations will know that the house of Israel went into exile because of their iniquity — because they were unfaithful to me, I hid my face from them.” The nations’ recognition has two components: (1) Israel’s exile was not YHWH’s defeat but his judgment; (2) YHWH’s restoration of Israel is an act of grace and power, not a capitulation to Babylonian pressure. The phrase “I hid my face from them” (veasater fanai meihem) is a significant OT theological concept: divine absence as judgment (Deuteronomy 31:17-18; Isaiah 54:8; Ezekiel 64:7). The hidden face is the OT equivalent of the experience of divine forsakenness.
24: “I dealt with them according to their uncleanness and their transgressions, and I hid my face from them.” The double reference to hiding YHWH’s face (Ezekiel 39:23-24) frames the exile as a period of divine absence — the opposite of the covenantal blessing of the Aaronic blessing (“YHWH make his face shine on you,” Numbers 6:25). The exile is the systematic reversal of the covenant blessings: land lost, divine presence withdrawn, nations contemptuous. Verse 24’s formula provides the theological basis for YHWH’s refusal to hide his face permanently (Ezekiel 39:29).
25: “Therefore thus says the Lord GOD: Now I will restore the fortunes of Jacob and have compassion on the whole house of Israel, and I will be zealous for my holy name.” The word “therefore” (lachen) here introduces not judgment (as in most of Ezekiel’s “therefore” formulas) but restoration — a significant reversal. “Restore the fortunes” (shavti et-shevut Yaakov): the term shevut (fortunes, captivity) plays on the root shuv (to return) — it is literally “I will return the returning of Jacob.” The jealousy for YHWH’s holy name (vekianti leshem qodshi) returns to the name-theology of Ezekiel 39:7 and Ezekiel 20: YHWH’s honor motivates restoration.
26: “After they have borne their shame and all their faithlessness by which they were unfaithful to me, when they dwell securely in their land with no one to make them afraid.” The “shame” (klimatam) Israel will bear is the same shame of post-restoration self-loathing promised in Ezekiel 20:43 (“you will loathe yourselves”). The bearing of shame is not ongoing condemnation but the natural response of a restored people who understand the gravity of their past unfaithfulness in light of YHWH’s grace. “Dwell securely in their land with no one to make them afraid” — the security formula contrasts with Leviticus 26:36 (covenant curse: “the sound of a driven leaf shall put them to flight”).
27: “When I have brought them back from the peoples and gathered them from the lands of their enemies, and through them have shown myself holy in the sight of many nations.” The gathering from the lands of enemies reverses the scattering of the Deuteronomic curses (Deuteronomy 28:64). The instrumental phrase “through them have shown myself holy” (veniqedashti vam) establishes that Israel’s restoration is itself a divine self-revelation event. The gathered, restored, secure Israel demonstrates YHWH’s holiness to the watching nations — making Israel’s very existence and restoration an ongoing witness to YHWH’s character.
28: “They will know that I am the LORD their God — I who sent them into exile among the nations, yet I have gathered them to their own land and left none of them behind.” The recognition formula here has a specific theological content: Israel will know that the same YHWH who sent them into exile has gathered them home. The exile and the return are both acts of the same sovereign God — the people will be unable to credit either event to chance, foreign gods, or historical accident. “Left none of them behind” (velo otir od meihem sham) — complete, comprehensive restoration, not a partial remnant return.
29: “I will no longer hide my face from them, for I have poured out my Spirit on the house of Israel, declares the Lord GOD.” The chapter’s climactic verse reverses the “hidden face” of Ezekiel 39:23-24 with a permanent declaration: the face-hiding is over. The ground of this permanent presence is the outpouring of YHWH’s Spirit (shafachti et-ruchi al-beit Yisrael). This is the Ezekielian counterpart to Joel 2:28-29 (“I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh”) and connects to Ezekiel 36:27 (“I will put my Spirit within you”) — the Spirit’s outpouring is the permanent seal of restoration, making YHWH’s presence with Israel irreversible. Peter cites Joel 2:28-29 at Pentecost as the beginning of this fulfillment (Acts 2:17-18).
